transcript.transcript: This is Julie Haas. Today is March 7, 1990. I'm visiting with Pat King in my home at 2804 North 9th Street in Coeur d'Alene, Idaho. And the purpose of our visit is to share memories and ideas about the changes in journalism and the newspaper business in Coeur d'Alene from the 1940s to the present. Pat King was employed by Haganon Newspapers for 40 years in many capacities and many locations. And I'd like to welcome you, Pat, and thank you for spending some time with me. And just to get started, I'd like you to tell us a little bit about your background. For example, where you were born, and what year was your birth, and the location of your birth, and perhaps even your parents' names. Are you ready, Julie? Thank you. Thanks for the introduction, and I'm a native North Idahoan, and I was born in Bonners Ferry on April 14th, 1923. My father was C.W. King, and they called him Charlie, and my mother was Alma S. King, and she was, her maiden name was Seidel, and she came from Iowa. And that's quite a background. My grandfather came from Germany about the time of Bismarck, and he didn't like the war and the fighting and the militarism of Germany at that time, and so he came to the United States and immediately wound up in the Civil War. But that's another story. Now, I went to school at Bonners Ferry, and grade school, and graduated from high school in 1941. And at that time, my father was the publisher of the Bonners Ferry Herald and owned the paper, and did a lot of job work in the paper, and that's probably where he made a great majority of his money, and because he operated the paper during the Depression, and in some good times too, but also was a instrumental in the Kootenai Valley development and the drainage to dike the Kootenai River so that all that farmland became a reality, and so they had quite a bit of farmland in the valley. So when I graduated from high school, I was debating on whether to go to journalism when I went to Idaho, which I'd always thought I would go, or to take agriculture and come, maybe come back and maybe have a chance to operate the farmlands that we had. Well, I took journalism and, And it's kind of a funny point, but I was ready to graduate in 48 after spending three years in the service. And my dad sold the paper about three months before I graduated. So I thought at that time, and I'm not sure whether I even told him that, I said I must have made a mistake and I should have gone into agriculture because the family still does have the farmland. And it's kind of ironic that Duane Haggedon now owns the Bonner's Ferry Herald, and I have worked for Dwayne and Burrough for over 40 years. That is an interesting and rich background that you have. Pat, who did you marry and what was your maiden name? And could you tell us how many children you have and what their names are? Well, I married Dorothy Neumeyer and she was a Bonners Ferry girl, but I, she was four years younger than I was, and she wasn't in high school when I was, but I met her when I came back from the war. And we had a quite a whirlwind courtship, and she was attending the Holy Names in Spokane, and I was at the University of Idaho, and about every week I'd get a ride up to Spokane or take the bus and, and, or once in a while she would be able to come down and see me at Moscow, but it was quite expensive. And so we finally decided to get married during the Christmas holidays in 1946. And her father and her family was a large family, and in fact, her sister lives here, and she has quite a few relatives, brothers and sisters in Bonners Ferry still. Our children are Chris King. She changed her name back from her married name and was divorced, and she works, she's assistant manager at the Pay and Pack. And my son Patrick is in Atlanta, Georgia, and he's a commander in the Navy and, and a, somewhat of a, he handles the reserve, and he's a pilot in the Naval Air Corps. And then Bob is a pressman with the Moses Lake paper. Columbia Basin Herald was, and so he works with, for Duane. Betsy is, lives here and, and works part-time at the hospital and manages the two boys. She lost her husband, as you know. And then we have Kevin, who's at the University of Idaho. And his major is music and music education. But he's now going to get a degree in business and finance. So he will probably have five or six years in college and he's the bright one in our family. He's always had excellent grades and really works at it. And Dan was a latecomer also, and he's a junior in high school, sophomore in high school, will be a junior at Hope, and he's kind of takes after his father and has never felt that he had to study or work at school. So that's been quite a problem for us. But he's probably a little better than the average high school student at this time. That gives the Obopta we have ten grandchildren. Some of the grandchildren are nearly as old as Dan. You've got quite a rich family there, Pat. Thank you. I had one question. Now, your wife's nickname is Chick. Right. Is that right? Mm-hmm. Was Chick in high school when you got married, or had she been in the military? Oh, no. No, she was in Holy Name. Oh, okay. She'd been, had one year. Okay. Thank you for clearing that up. Did you have any other jobs before entering the field of journalism? Part-time jobs or summer jobs? Well, I worked at, I worked on the, in the farm, all pretty nearly every year, but I also worked every year, nights and Saturdays and Sundays at the Bonner Sherry Herald, doing mostly job work. I never did get into writing or that, but Stan had quite a job in. In fact, he produced the telephone directories for, well, it is General Telephone now, but it was an interstate telephone at that time. And for our listeners sake, job work is just that, publishing for printing? Printing. For other businesses, is that it? Mm-hmm. And then I worked at the, on the farm in the summer, and that was out about 25 or 30 miles from Bonnechere North and on the Kootenai River, so we didn't get in very often. Herded sheep and milk cows and probably about a 14 or 15 hour day. And would you like to know how much we made? I think our listeners would. $15 a month. Oh my. And that was, like I say, we worked from 6 o'clock in the morning until probably 6 o'clock at night, and our main enjoyment was to go and have a swim in the Kootenai River. So did you find very many places to spend your money if you lived that far out? No. No, it wasn't any place. And we didn't get into town very often either. How often did you get into town? Oh, maybe twice a month and, what was it? How far did you move from school? Oh, not very far. About a half mile. I walked to school. And I have fond memories of when I was a senior in high school, and this is something different. Nowadays, I talk to my brother had a model of the 1932 Chevrolet. And he let me use it. And I came down to the basketball tournament here at Coeur d'Alene by myself, as well as some other high school seniors, you know. And, but I had to give the keys when I arrived here to Mrs. Pace, who was the wife of the superintendent of schools at Bonner's, and so I didn't get those keys until Sunday morning when the tournament was over, and the tournament was held in the Elks Club at that time. Were you a participant? No, I was an observer. That sounds to me like it was a big privilege to take the car and come down here for the weekend. Well, I thought it was, but I couldn't use it. One other time when I was younger, I came to a tournament with my mother and we stayed at the Desert Hotel, which I don't know if you remember or not. And where was that located in Cornell? Well, for security, as they burned down, was it 69, 70? It's on the, it's right down there in my office. I can't even, I can't remember what the date is. Anyway, maybe we can clear that up later, but I stayed, we stayed at the hotel, and I contacted the Measles. So I knew that I wasn't feeling very well, but I wanted to see the championship game. I think it was 39, because Bonners was in the championship, and in fact I think we won it. But, so I stayed away from my mother a whole day Saturday. I just, and went to the game and she saw me sitting over there in the student section, my bright red face and everything. So I don't know how many people I gave the measles to. And you were in high school at the time? Let's see, I, well, yeah, well, no, I can't. I don't think I was. I think I was probably 12 or 13, so that'd be 35 or 36. So your trips to Coeur d'Alene were memorable, it sounds to me like. Well, I'll tell you one more. My, this guy got to be about 10 or 12. My father went to Boise and took me with him. And on our way back, he had to stop and see Mr. Haggedon and I was quite impressed and so that was when the building was on North 4th. So I was, dad introduced me to Burl and I remember going into that office with no idea that I'd ever be working for him. I was quite impressed with the plant. It was, you know, it was quite a little bit bigger than the Bonner Sherry Herald, and I'm pretty sure this was Idaho Press Association businesses dad and Burrow were talking about. My dad was a state senator and was the youngest state senator in Idaho. I didn't realize that. And what year was that? 23. And how long did you serve? Just one term. I think that's all I said. Getting back a little bit into the idea of journalism, Did you enjoy writing in high school? I didn't have time. I, well, I enjoyed writing, but I didn't have much time. I was busy with, with many other, I did work on the, on the annual and wrote for that, but I didn't, I was too busy in athletics and I wasn't good at it, but I, in fact, when I was a freshman and sophomore I was the manager and tried to be the manager of the basketball team. I did participate when I was a senior. I think I weighed 125 then. And what sports did you participate in? Oh, football and basketball. Of course we didn't have baseball. That was probably my, I was probably the best in baseball. I'm trying to find out a little bit about how you became interested in journalism or writing. I was wondering if there was one teacher who left an impression on you or stirred a desire in you to write. Did you approach journalism from that standpoint, Or was it from the standpoint of your father? From my father, I think. So there was not a teacher who left an impression. Mr. Spellman was probably the only one that I can really remember, except Mr. Sauerling. I don't know if you've come in contact with Mr. Sauerling. He lived here for quite a few years. He's still living, but he is in very good condition. And in what way did he leave an imprint? Well, he was just such a fine man. In fact, I paled with the superintendent's son. And Russ Sartling came from Minnesota and came up to their place in about August and he was there to take the job of a math teacher and and principal of the high school I believe. And I was impressed with him, always was, and he's a fine man. What influenced you to move to Coeur d'Alene? Were you transferred here or did you choose to move here? Chose to move here. And what year was that? 1948. And what was the reason that you moved here? Well, if you want a long story, I was working at the Walla Walla Union Bulletin and I was at that time one of the few that they, times that they started doing this is they had a, one of their people come to the university and, and interview students that were going to graduate for jobs. And I interviewed and got a job with them. As an advertising salesperson. So was that your first job out of college? Except I was, and I was worried about it, I was supposed to, well no, I was, they had, it was in 48, We had a, that was one of the worst floods that they'd had in Kootenai Valley, and also the whole Northwest. And I went up to help out at the, between finishing my classwork and graduation exercises. And I got stuck out on our district. District 13 was our district. And couldn't get out of there. And I was worried because all the other districts had flooded, and finally the Army engineers came in with a, with one of these boats, and we got out that way, and then in Bonners, by the time I got in there, Bonners Shrew was flooded, though, and the dikes had in fact come in from the bottom, flooded back in, back with water. So I was worried whether I'd even get to Moscow for my graduation, but I did. And then immediately Chick and I took off for Walla Walla, and it was all flooded down there from all the streams down there were flooded, so we had a heck of a time. Well, how long did you live in Walla Walla? Just about six months. And then you moved to Portland? Mm-hmm. Burrell's brother, Sully, was a friend of mine. I don't know if you... He's dead now, but... I don't think our listeners know... Roger. Roger and he was a younger brother. Roger Hagedorn? Mm-hmm. And he was the owner of... He wasn't an owner, He was a, he was in radio in Walla Walla, and he worked part-time in a service station, and Chick and I rented a duplex right across from the service station. So I would go over there in the evening and talk to him. I ran into him. So he told me that his brother had an opening in the advertising department at Coraline Press. So I called and got an interview, and in fact I rode up with Roger for this interview. And about what month was that? August. August of 1948. And then did you move to Coeur d'Alene right away after that? Probably about, I think it was around middle of September. Before we go on with your, the discussion of your job in Coeur d'Alene in 1948, I'd like to backtrack a little bit. You have kind of an interesting background in your approach to education at the University of Idaho and the type of degree that you got. Would you share that with us? Well, I was always interested in being a manager of a newspaper. And I didn't, I had to take the journalism courses in writing and all courses, but Professor Scott, who was the head of the journalism department, programmed a curriculum for me that would include management and business courses that would help me in managing and operating a newspaper. And I still have the books. But there were special classes, of course, like I said, I had to take everything to get my degree, but he was very instrumental in providing work for me. He also had me teach classes in advertising and management, which I have a few friends here in Coeur d'Alene that I had in those classes. Were you a college student at the time when you taught those classes. And how many other people got this kind of a degree? I don't think I know of anybody that was that, of that interest. And what was the type of degree? What would it be? Well, I still got a BA in journalism. I see. With an emphasis in business? Journalism. I took business law and accounting and business courses also so that I would have some background in the operation of a newspaper. That's interesting. I think that helps our listeners have an understanding of the broad base of your knowledge and preparation for what you've done. Where was the Coeur d'Alene Press building located in 1948 when you first started working for the paper? Second and Lakeside and the address is 201 North 2nd, I believe. Now is that the present location? Yes. How have the facilities changed since then? Oh, drastically. I think it's been remodeled five times since I've started there, and It was just, it was an old grocery store and warehouse when Burl purchased. And it had just been about a, probably six months before I arrived, it had just been changed and built for a, for a newspaper facility. But then there was many changes over the years that I was here that remodeling. There had to be. Right. So, for our listeners' sake, could you tell us who the owner and publisher was at the time? Burl Hagadon was both owner and publisher. Burl was somewhat... Had the same philosophy as I did and he started out as a advertising salesman and stayed in business Didn't very seldom wrote articles And that's where I kind of followed in his footsteps. And Dwayne's pretty much the same. Can you share some of your memories of Burl Hagedorn with us? He was different than Dwayne by quite a bit. He didn't associate with the employees very much. You know, you wonder why he didn't, but he was very intelligent and ran an excellent operation. He was tight with the dollar, And I'll tell you a story on that. When I was advertising manager, we could hear, you know, when Ann Newman was the business manager in the front office, and I heard Burrell say, who in the world authorized this bill? And Ann said, Pat. Gene, Pat did. He said, send him in. So I went quaking in to find out what Bill he was talking about and what it was. I think I probably taught myself out of the jeopardy, but Burroughs is quite tight. I mean, we would never have, in my estimation, gone as far as we have under Duane's leadership. Duane was more outgoing and wasn't afraid to take risks. I'm not sure that Burroughs was afraid, but I think he didn't want to put himself in jeopardy financially. Right. That comment makes me want to ask so many questions at one time. At the time that Burl was publisher and part owner? He was the owner primarily. Now Scripps and its Scripps League was a partner. I see. And in many cases they would buy the paper and then take a local person in with them. But I think Burl probably had, probably, well I know that Burl had more, the, over the 60% of the ownership of the paper, so I think that makes him owner, doesn't it? Pretty much. Technically I'm not sure about that. Then at the time the Cornell Ink Press was in the Scripps League, and how many other papers were in the league at the time? Do you remember? I would say 35 to 36 papers they had. They're all about the same size, some some larger. Tacoma and Klamath Falls. And did Burl have any say or decision making in the running of the other papers? No, but he did now see he was general manager of the Kalispell paper and also Santa Maria I think was the one that Burrell was, and a couple, three others. In fact, Burrell was the, was the, what you call, general manager at the Tacoma Tribune, I think it was the Tribune. Do you remember what was circulation 40 years ago when you first started with the press? Probably 5, 000. Were there any other newspapers, Was there a competing newspaper in the city at the time? There was a Kootenai County Leader, and that was a weekly. It was a pretty good little paper, and I can't think of the name of the publisher except then Joe Whitley, or Whiteley, bought him out in the late 50s. And I asked Chick last night, and then I should have looked it up, but I didn't know where to. That's fine. That's a detail that we can... I can find for you. Okay. Was the Coeur d'Alene Press a daily at the time that this other paper was a weekly? Yeah. At that time, was a Spokane paper also considered a competitor? You've mentioned one that was a local competitor, and I'm just wondering at that time if Spokane started the inroads as far as circulation in the Coeur d'Alene area. They did circulation-wise, but not extensively. I would think, well, the Spokesman Review and the Spokane Chronicle, the Chronicle was probably more dominant at that time than the Review. I don't know, I don't know. There's more people took the clinical, but in my estimation, anyway. The review was, was, came in North Idaho, but they didn't feature North Idaho news. You know, they'd had correspondents that would send news in, but I mean it wasn't like they were doing at this time. What kinds of campaigns were successful in boosting circulation back in the 40s since there was, you might say, a competitive region in town? Carrier boys contest, primarily. And the carrier boys, you know, would work nearly every Saturday going out with prizes and games and foods and picnics and etc. That was the big picnics, etc. That was the big push for subscribers. When did the press cease to be part of the Skirt League? 1976. That's what I would say, anyway. I wasn't here then.

lines.line: # Introduction and Purpose

> Julie Haas introduces herself and the purpose of the interview with Pat King, discussing changes in journalism and the newspaper business in Coeur d'Alene from the 1940s onwards. Pat King is welcomed to share his background and career in journalism.
** introduction, purpose, journalism, newspaper business, Coeur d'Alene, Pat King **
